---
title: "From Raster to Portal: Publishing Biodiversity Data as an EBV NetCDF"
subtitle: "A NaturaConnect tutorial on the ebvcube R package and the EBV Data Portal"
---

## Why this tutorial exists

Biodiversity data is only useful to decision-makers if it can be found, trusted, and compared across studies. That is the problem **Essential Biodiversity Variables (EBVs)** were designed to solve: a common language and file format so that a species-distribution model from one team and a community-composition model from another can sit side by side on the same map.

This tutorial walks you through the full path from "I have some raster outputs on my laptop" to "my dataset is live on the EBV Data Portal" — using a real, published dataset as the running example, so every step produces something you can check against the real thing.

By the end, you will be able to:

- explain what an EBV is and why the EBV netCDF format looks the way it does;
- install and configure the `ebvcube` R package;
- prepare your own raster/species data for conversion;
- build a valid EBV netCDF with `ebv_create()` and `ebv_add_data()`;
- validate the file before submitting it;
- upload and publish the dataset on the [EBV Data Portal](https://portal.geobon.org/);
- troubleshoot the errors you are most likely to hit along the way.

## What is an EBV, in one paragraph

An **Essential Biodiversity Variable** is a measurement standardized well enough to be tracked consistently over time, across regions, and across research groups — the biodiversity equivalent of an Essential Climate Variable. GEO BON groups them into six classes (genetic composition, species populations, species traits, community composition, ecosystem structure, ecosystem function), and every EBV dataset shares the same underlying file format: a CF- and ACDD-compliant **netCDF** with up to four dimensions — longitude, latitude, time, and entity (species, group, or ecosystem type). That shared format is what lets the [EBV Data Portal](https://portal.geobon.org/) host, filter, and compare hundreds of otherwise very different datasets.

## The dataset we'll use

Rather than a toy example, this tutorial anchors its portal-facing sections to a real, already-published dataset:

> **Global trends in biodiversity (BES-SIM cSAR-iDiv)** — Inês Martins & Henrique Pereira (2022). Community-composition projections from 1900–2050 under multiple SSP/RCP scenarios, produced for the BES-SIM inter-model comparison for IPBES.
> [Dataset page](https://portal.geobon.org/ebv-detail?id=30) · DOI: [10.25829/5zmy41](https://doi.org/10.25829/5zmy41) · netCDF download: 3.27 MB

We use this dataset (portal ID 30) whenever we're exploring the *portal* — its metadata form, entity list, DOI, citation, and versioning — so everything you see corresponds to something you can open yourself, right now, on the live portal.

For the *hands-on R code*, we mostly use the small example file bundled with the `ebvcube` package (`martins_comcom_subset.nc`), so every chunk runs instantly with no download. That file is a related but **different** dataset by the same author and model family — [portal ID 1, "Local bird diversity (cSAR/BES-SIM)"](https://portal.geobon.org/ebv-detail?id=1), a spatial (African) subset of a 1900–2015 land-use-driven bird diversity product. Unit 3 shows how to download the actual dataset‑30 netCDF (small enough, at 3.27 MB, to work with directly) if you want to run the full workflow against it end-to-end instead.

## The workflow at a glance

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["1. Prepare your data\n(rasters + metadata)"] --> B["2. Generate the metadata JSON\n(portal form or ebv_metadata())"]
    B --> C["3. ebv_create()\nempty, valid netCDF skeleton"]
    C --> D["4. ebv_add_data()\nfill in each entity / time step"]
    D --> E["5. Validate\nebv_properties(), Panoply, portal validator"]
    E --> F["6. Upload to the\nEBV Data Portal"]
    F --> G["7. Review & publish\n(DOI assigned)"]
```

## How this tutorial is organized

| Unit | Topic |
|:---|:---|
| 1 | Introduction to EBVs and the EBV Data Portal |
| 2 | Installing R, RStudio, and the `ebvcube` package |
| 3 | Preparing your biodiversity data for conversion |
| 4 | Creating an EBV netCDF with `ebvcube` |
| 5 | Validating the generated file |
| 6 | Uploading your dataset to the EBV Data Portal |
| 7 | Troubleshooting common issues and best practices |
| 8 | Further resources, repositories, and references |

Each unit opens with its learning objectives, works through the step with runnable code and screenshots, and closes with a short checkpoint so you know you're ready to move on.

## Before you start

You don't need prior netCDF experience — Unit 1 covers the concepts you need. You should be comfortable with:

- basic R (installing packages, running scripts);
- basic geospatial data in R (`terra`/raster basics);
- having your own species or biodiversity raster data ready, or being happy to follow along with the example dataset.

## Key resources

- **EBV Data Portal** — <https://portal.geobon.org/> — discover, download, and publish EBV datasets.
- **`ebvcube` R package** — <https://github.com/EBVcube/ebvcube> — read, visualize, and create EBV netCDFs. Also on [CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/package=ebvcube) and the [b-cubed r-universe](https://b-cubed-eu.r-universe.dev/ebvcube).
- **`ebvcube` documentation book** — <https://EBVcube.github.io/ebvcube/> — the full function reference this tutorial builds on.
- **EBV netCDF format specification** — <https://github.com/EBVcube/EBVCube-format> — the structural rules behind the format.
- **GEO BON — What are EBVs?** — <https://geobon.org/ebvs/what-are-ebvs/> — the canonical definition and the six EBV classes.
- **Methods paper** — Quoss et al. (2022), describing the package and the netCDF structure it implements.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Working alongside this tutorial.** Every code chunk is runnable as-is against the bundled example dataset. If you're preparing your *own* dataset for the portal, follow the same steps but substitute your rasters and metadata where indicated in Units 3–4.
:::

Ready? Start with [Unit 1 — Introduction to EBVs and the EBV Data Portal](01-introduction.qmd).
